test_id_55.answers:
{
   "headline_summary": "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective 2023-05-01) and explicitly states that home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with Medicare Part D as primary where applicable.",
   "policy_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to explicitly define home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities. According to the newest policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10\" effective 2023-05-01, there is discussion regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs (PADs), including responsibilities for replacement if improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen, and concerns about 'Brown bagging' [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:12 — 2023-05-01]. It also mentions general \"Policy and Billing\" topics and directs FFS claim and policy questions to specific contacts [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:2, 3 — 2021-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:12, 13 — 2023-05-01]. However, these excerpts do not specifically address the coverage or billing responsibilities for home infusion drugs.",
   "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, \"New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines\" effective 2025-10-01, the New York State Medicaid Program \"does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Instead, \"Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part D, and Medicare benefits \"must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01]. Federal regulations require that all available third-party resources be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01].",
   "similarities": [
      "Both sources, in their general context, refer to the existence of billing guidelines and the need for providers to seek specific information for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:12, 13 — 2023-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].",
      "Both sources implicitly or explicitly acknowledge the hierarchy of payment, with the provider manual explicitly stating that Medicare and other third-party benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17, 18 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "differences": [
      "The provider manual, effective 2025-10-01, explicitly defines the coverage and billing for home infusion drugs, stating that there is no bundled payment and that drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, with Medicare Part D as a primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].",
      "The policy documents (newest effective 2023-05-01), in the provided excerpts, do not contain any specific information regarding the coverage or billing responsibilities for home infusion drugs, focusing instead on general drug administration and billing topics for other services like Practitioner Administered Drugs (PADs) [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:12 — 2023-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:2, 3 — 2021-08-01]."
   ],
   "caveats": "The provider manual is significantly more recent (effective 2025-10-01) and provides direct, explicit guidance on the coverage and billing of home infusion drugs, including the non-bundled payment approach and the role of Medicare Part D and other third-party payers. The policy documents, in the provided excerpts, do not offer any specific information on this topic. Therefore, the provider manual should be considered the authoritative source for current guidance on home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities."
}

policy.text:
May 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Reminder: Substance Use Disorder Drug Treatment:
Medicaid Managed Care and Fee-for-Service Coverage
Important Legislation Regarding Drugs to Treat Substance Use Disorders:
• Medicaid policy requires Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) to provide
at least five days’ coverage for emergencies, without prior authorization, for medications used to
treat substance use disorders. This includes medication associated with the management of opioid
withdrawal and/or stabilization as well as medication used for opioid overdose reversal.
• As published in the August 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update, New York State Social Services Law
§364-j and Public Health Law §273, prohibit prior authorization under Medicaid FFS and MMC for
initial or renewal prescriptions for preferred or formulary forms of buprenorphine or injectable
naltrexone when used for detoxification or maintenance treatment of opioid addiction. The
frequency, quantity, and/or duration limits supported by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and
Compendia may continue to be applied.

Questions and Additional Information:
NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program:
• NYS Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at
(800) 343-9000.
• NYS Medicaid FFS coverage and policy questions should be directed to the Office of Health Insurance
Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management (DPDM) by telephone at
(518) 473-2160 or by email at FFSMedicaidPolicy@health.ny.gov.
• NYRx coverage and policy questions should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit by
telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 12

MMC:
• MMC general coverage questions should be directed to the OHIP Division of Health Plan Contracting
and Oversight (DHPCO) by telephone at (518) 473-1134 or by email at covques@health.ny.gov.
• MMC reimbursement, billing, and/or documentation requirement questions should be directed to the
MMC Plan of the enrollee.
• MMC Plan contact information can be found in the eMedNY New York Stat

NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided

federal legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to Social Security Act
(SSA) §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and Social Services Law (SOS) §365-a(4)(f), located
at: https://www.nysenate.gov/legis

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
For handwritten orders, a rubber stamp or other mechanical signature device may not be used.
Prior authorization must also be obtained for certain brand name drugs.
Additionally, in order to dispense a brand name drug when the prescriber indicates “DAW” and, indicates “Brand
Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” on the prescription, the pharmacist must indicate a “yes” in the brand
necessary field of the paper claim form or when billing electronically, refer to the NCPDP D.0 Companion Guide for one
of the listed codes for submission in field 408-D8- (Dispense As Written (DAW)/Product Selection Code).
For more information, refer to: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-
ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
• Any contrast agents, used for radiological testing (these are included in the radiologist’s fee).
• Drugs packaged in unit doses for which bulk product exists.
*Some exceptions apply, see Unapproved Drugs

{
   "headline_summary": "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective 2025-01-01). Both sources indicate that reimbursement for drugs, including naloxone, is contingent upon inclusion on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, with an older policy document specifically noting emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications.",
   "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1\" effective 2025-01-01, non-prescription drugs with their 11-digit NDCs are reimbursable under NYRx if they appear on the \"New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11 — 2025-01-01]. An older policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10\" effective 2020-05-01, specifies that Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) must provide at least five days’ coverage for emergencies, without prior authorization, for medications used to treat substance use disorders [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7 — 2020-05-01].",
   "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, \"New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines\" effective 2025-10-01, Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32, 33 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that registered pharmacists, certified by the NYS Education Department, may administer certain long-acting injectable (LAI) drugs for the treatment of mental health or substance abuse based on a patient-specific prescription or order after the initial injection was provided in a practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. However, the provided excerpts do not explicitly detail specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone or emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications.",
   "similarities": [
      "Both the newest policy document (2025-01-01) and the newest provider manual (2025-10-01) state that reimbursement for drugs is contingent upon their inclusion on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11 — 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].",
      "Both sources generally address drug coverage within the NYS Medicaid program, including aspects related to substance use disorders [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7 — 2020-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "differences": [
      "The policy documents, specifically an older one (effective 2020-05-01), explicitly state that medications used to treat substance use disorders, which would include naloxone, must receive at least five days’ emergency coverage without prior authorization for both FFS and MMC [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7 — 2020-05-01].",
      "The newest policy document (effective 2025-01-01) specifically mentions that non-prescription drugs with 11-digit NDCs are reimbursable if on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11 — 2025-01-01].",
      "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) does not explicitly detail emergency coverage or specific provisions for naloxone or non-prescription SUD drugs in the provided excerpts, focusing instead on general drug list requirements and pharmacist administration of long-acting injectable (LAI) drugs for substance abuse *treatment* [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 28, 32, 33 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "caveats": "The provider manual is more recent overall (effective 2025-10-01) and provides the general framework that drugs must be on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" for reimbursement. However, an older policy document (effective 2020-05-01) offers crucial, specific guidance regarding emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications like naloxone (at least five days, no prior authorization). While the provider manual does not contradict this, it does not explicitly reiterate these specific conditions in the provided excerpts. Therefore, for the specific *conditions* of naloxone reimbursement, particularly emergency access, the older policy document provides more direct and detailed guidance, which is not superseded by the general statements in the newer provider manual."
}

Vaccine Administration for Adults and Non-VFC Program Vaccines for Children Under 19 Years of

Vaccinations for NYS Medicaid members 19 years of age and older are reimbursed via APGs. Providers are
to bill the vaccine/toxoid CPT code administered to receive the APG line-item reimbursement. A separate
reimbursement, as per the NYS DOH APG-Based Weights History File, located at: https://view.officeapps.live.
com/op/view.aspx?src=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.health.ny.gov%2Fhealth_care%2Fmedicaid%2Frates%2Fm
ethodology%2Fdocs%2Fhistory_and_fee_schedule.xls&wdOrigin=BROWSELINK, will be made for the
administration of the influenza and/or pneumococcal vaccine for adults 19 years of age and older when the
following vaccine administration codes are added to the claim:
• Influenza – “G0008”
• Pneumococcal – “G0009”
All other vaccinations administered to NYS Medicaid members, 19 years of age and older, and vaccines for
NYS Medicaid members under the age of 19 years not covered by the VFC program (e.g. travel-related

   "headline_summary": "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective 2025-01-01). Both sources indicate that reimbursement for drugs, including naloxone, is contingent upon inclusion on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, with an older policy document specifically noting emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications.",
   "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1\" effective 2025-01-01, non-prescription drugs with their 11-digit NDCs are reimbursable under NYRx if they appear on the \"New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11 — 2025-01-01]. An older policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10\" effective 2020-05-01, specifies that Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) must provide at least five days’ coverage for emergencies, without prior authorization, for medications used to treat substance use disorders [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7 — 2020-05-01].",
   "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, \"New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines\" effective 2025-10-01, Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32, 33 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that registered pharmacists, certified by the NYS Education Department, may administer certain long-acting injectable (LAI) drugs for the treatment of mental health or substance abuse based on a patient-specific prescription or order after the initial injection was provided in a practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. However, the provided excerpts do not explicitly detail specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone or emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications.",
   "similarities": [
      "Both the newest policy document (effective 2025-01-01) and the newest provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) state that reimbursement for drugs is contingent upon their inclusion on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11 — 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].",
      "Both sources generally address drug coverage within the NYS Medicaid program, including aspects related to substance use disorders [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7 — 2020-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "differences": [
      "The policy documents, specifically an older one (effective 2020-05-01), explicitly state that medications used to treat substance use disorders, which would include naloxone, must receive at least five days’ emergency coverage without prior authorization for both FFS and MMC [New York State Medicaid Update May 2020 Volume 36 Number 10:7 — 2020-05-01].",
      "The newest policy document (effective 2025-01-01) specifically mentions that non-prescription drugs with 11-digit NDCs are reimbursable if on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:11 — 2025-01-01].",
      "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) does not explicitly detail emergency coverage or specific provisions for naloxone or non-prescription SUD drugs in the provided excerpts, focusing instead on general drug list requirements and pharmacist administration of long-acting injectable (LAI) drugs for substance abuse *treatment* [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 28, 32, 33 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "caveats": "The provider manual is more recent overall (effective 2025-10-01) and provides the general framework that drugs must be on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" for reimbursement. However, an older policy document (effective 2020-05-01) offers crucial, specific guidance regarding emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications like naloxone (at least five days, no prior authorization). While the provider manual does not contradict this, it does not explicitly reiterate these specific conditions in the provided excerpts. Therefore, for the specific *conditions* of naloxone reimbursement, particularly emergency access, the older policy document provides more direct and detailed guidance, which is not superseded by the general statements in the newer provider manual."
}

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2021 Volume 37 Number 6

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
For handwritten orders, a rubber stamp or other mechanical signature device may not be used.
Prior authorization must also be obtained for certain brand name drugs.
Additionally, in order to dispense a brand name drug when the prescriber indicates “DAW” and, indicates “Brand
Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” on the prescription, the pharmacist must indicate a “yes” in the brand
necessary field of the paper claim form or when billing electronically, refer to the NCPDP D.0 Companion Guide for one
of the listed codes for submission in field 408-D8- (Dispense As Written (DAW)/Product Selection Code).
For more information, refer to: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-
ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacists Administering Mental Health or Substance Abuse Treatment Injections at the Pharmacy
Registered pharmacists, certified by the NYS Education Department, may administer a long-acting injectable (LAI)
approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of mental health or substance abuse based on a
patient specific prescription or order after the initial injection was provided in the practitioner’s office.
Policy
• The certified pharmacist must confirm the initial injection has been given by the practitioner and the
member is ready for maintenance therapy.
• The member or member’s representative must consent to the administration by a certified pharmacist.
• The pharmacy must provide a private area for the administration.
• The certified pharmacist must notify the member’s prescriber of the drug administration, and if there were
adverse reactions or any side effects.
Billing Guidelines
Pharmacies will be reimbursed for the medication and an administration fee when billed to NYRx. The member will not
have a copayment for drug administration.
• NYS legislation requires a patient specific prescription or order for administration. These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
• Any contrast agents, used for radiological testing (these are included in the radiologist’s fee).
• Drugs packaged in unit doses for which bulk product exists.
*Some exceptions apply, see Unapproved Drugs

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescription Drugs
Pharmacy reimbursement for prescription drugs under the New York State Medicaid Program is established in law.
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
** See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
National Average Drug Acquisition Cost (NADAC) is determined by a federal survey and is an average of the drug
acquisition costs submitted by retail community pharmacies.
State Maximum Acquisition Cost (SMAC) is developed by Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions for NY
Medicaid and is applied on multiple source generic drugs. It represents an upper limit that NY Medicaid will pay for
these drugs.
Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) is the lowest net charge to the general public/cash customers on the date of
provision of service, not to exceed the lower sale price, if any, in effect on that date.
Wholesaler Acquisition Cost (WAC) is an estimate of the manufacturer’s list price for a drug to wholesalers or other
direct purchasers, not including discounts or rebates. The price is defined by federal law.
The NY Medicaid Pharmacy Benefit Manager will receive all requests from pharmacy providers concerning the validity
of a SMAC price. The SMAC Research Request Form is posted on the Pharmacy Benefit Manager’s web site at:
https://newyork.fhsc.com/providers/smacinfo.asp.
Questions from pharmacy providers concerning the validity of a SMAC price should be referred to the NY Medicaid
Clinical Call Center at (877)309-9493.
Note: the Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.

{
   "headline_summary": "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) is more recent than the policy documents (newest effective 2024-10-01). Neither source, in the provided excerpts, explicitly details specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone or emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications, though the provider manual outlines general drug reimbursement principles.",
   "policy_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to explicitly define specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone. The newest policy document, \"New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11\" effective 2024-10-01, discusses reimbursement for vaccine administration via APGs [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11:8 — 2024-10-01]. Older policy documents mention general billing guidelines for practitioner-administered drugs and contact information for FFS questions [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:12, 13 — 2023-05-01] and discuss FFS-approved drug reimbursement for brand name drugs like SUBOXONE® [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:4 — 2021-08-01]. However, none of these excerpts explicitly address naloxone reimbursement.",
   "provider_manual_definition": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to explicitly define specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone. According to the newest provider manual, \"New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines\" effective 2025-10-01, Medicaid only provides reimbursement for multiple source drugs contained on the \"List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32, 33 — 2025-10-01]. Pharmacy reimbursement for prescription drugs is established in law with a systematic pricing methodology based on drug type (Generics, Brands, OTCs) and factors like NADAC, FUL, WAC, SMAC, and U&C, along with a professional dispensing fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31, 32 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that registered pharmacists, certified by the NYS Education Department, may administer certain long-acting injectable (LAI) drugs for the treatment of mental health or substance abuse based on a patient-specific prescription or order after the initial injection was provided in a practitioner’s office [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01]. However, these excerpts do not explicitly detail specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone or emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications.",
   "similarities": [
      "Both sources are official NYS Medicaid documents that address aspects of drug coverage and reimbursement within the program [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11:8 — 2024-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31, 32, 33 — 2025-10-01].",
      "Both sources implicitly or explicitly refer to established guidelines or lists for drug reimbursement, with the provider manual explicitly mentioning the \"List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" and the policy documents referring to \"FFS-approved drug reimbursement\" for certain drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32, 33 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:4 — 2021-08-01]."
   ],
   "differences": [
      "The provider manual (effective 2025-10-01) explicitly outlines general drug reimbursement principles, including the requirement for drugs to be on the \"List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" and a systematic pricing methodology for prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:31, 32, 33 — 2025-10-01]. It also discusses pharmacist administration of LAI drugs for mental health or substance abuse *treatment* [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — 2025-10-01].",
      "The policy documents (newest effective 2024-10-01), in the provided excerpts, do not offer general drug reimbursement principles or a comprehensive list of reimbursable drugs, focusing instead on specific topics like vaccine administration or general billing inquiries [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11:8 — 2024-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:12, 13 — 2023-05-01].",
      "Neither source, in the provided excerpts, explicitly details specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone or emergency coverage for substance use disorder medications [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11:8 — 2024-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15, 31, 32, 33 — 2025-10-01]."
   ],
   "caveats": "The provider manual is more recent (effective 2025-10-01) and provides general drug reimbursement principles, such as the requirement for drugs to be on the \"New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs\" and a pricing methodology. However, neither the provider manual nor the policy documents, in the provided excerpts, explicitly detail specific reimbursement provisions for naloxone, such as emergency coverage or specific billing codes for overdose reversal. Therefore, while the provider manual offers general drug reimbursement context, specific guidance for naloxone reimbursement is not present in these excerpts."
}

• NYRx coverage and policy questions should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit by
telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 12

MMC:
• MMC general coverage questions should be directed to the OHIP Division of Health Plan Contracting
and Oversight (DHPCO) by telephone at (518) 473-1134 or by email at covques@health.ny.gov.
• MMC reimbursement, billing, and/or documentation requirement questions should be directed to the
MMC Plan of the enrollee.
• MMC Plan contact information can be found in the eMedNY New York Stat
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacists Administering Mental Health or Substance Abuse Treatment Injections at the Pharmacy
Registered pharmacists, certified by the NYS Education Department, may administer a long-acting injectable (LAI)
approved by the Food and Drug Administration for the treatment of mental health or substance abuse based on a
patient specific prescription or order after the initial injection was provided in the practitioner’s office.
Policy
• The certified pharmacist must confirm the initial injection has been given by the practitioner and the
member is ready for maintenance therapy.
• The member or member’s representative must consent to the administration by a certified pharmacist.
• The pharmacy must provide a private area for the administration.
• The certified pharmacist must notify the member’s prescriber of the drug administration, and if there were
adverse reactions or any side effects.
Billing Guidelines
Pharmacies will be reimbursed for the medication and an administration fee when billed to NYRx. The member will not
have a copayment for drug administration.
• NYS legislation requires a patient specific prescription or order for administration. These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
For handwritten orders, a rubber stamp or other mechanical signature device may not be used.
Prior authorization must also be obtained for certain brand name drugs.
Additionally, in order to dispense a brand name drug when the prescriber indicates “DAW” and, indicates “Brand
Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” on the prescription, the pharmacist must indicate a “yes” in the brand
necessary field of the paper claim form or when billing electronically, refer to the NCPDP D.0 Companion Guide for one
of the listed codes for submission in field 408-D8- (Dispense As Written (DAW)/Product Selection Code).
For more information, refer to: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-
ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
• Any contrast agents, used for radiological testing (these are included in the radiologist’s fee).
• Drugs packaged in unit doses for which bulk product exists.
*Some exceptions apply, see Unapproved Drugs
